_title: BS 7671:2018+A2:2022 Regulation 434 — Protection Against Fault Current
_note: Verifies that the cable can survive a short-circuit fault for the time the protective device takes to clear it. The adiabatic equation must be satisfied for every circuit.

principle.purpose: During a fault, very high current flows for a short time before the device trips. The cable's conductor temperature must stay below the limit that damages the insulation, even momentarily.
principle.assumption: Heat generated during fault is too rapid to dissipate — all heat stays in the conductor (adiabatic conditions). True for fault durations ≤ 5 seconds.

S.name: Cable cross-sectional area
S.description: Conductor size — line, neutral, or CPC depending on the fault path being analysed.

I.name: Effective fault current
I.description: RMS prospective short-circuit current at the fault location. Use the worst case — typically calculated at the source end (highest fault) and the load end (longest disconnection time).

t.name: Operating time of the protective device
t.description: From the device's time/current characteristic at current I. For instantaneous trip (MCB above Ia): use 0.1s typical.

k.name: Material/insulation constant
k.description: Depends on conductor material AND insulation type. See table below.

adiabatic_equation.interpretation: If S < √(I²t)/k, the cable will be damaged during fault clearance. Increase cable size, fit faster device, or reduce fault current with upstream impedance.

k_values._note: k value depends on conductor material, insulation, and the cable's role (line conductor vs CPC vs separate earth). The CPC may have a different k because its operating temperature differs.

line_conductors_initial_70C._basis: Tables 43.1 and 54.2 — line conductor at 70°C maximum (PVC insulated cable)

line_conductors_initial_90C._basis: Tables 43.1 and 54.2 — line conductor at 90°C maximum (XLPE/EPR insulated cable)

cpc_within_cable_pvc._basis: Table 54.3 — CPC inside cable with PVC sheath. CPC runs cool relative to line conductors.

cpc_within_cable_xlpe._basis: Table 54.3 — CPC inside cable with thermosetting sheath

cpc_separate_pvc._basis: Table 54.4 — separate CPC (e.g. single conductor in conduit) with PVC insulation

cpc_separate_xlpe._basis: Table 54.4 — separate CPC with thermosetting insulation

bare_cpc_in_air._basis: Table 54.5 — bare conductor used as CPC (e.g. earth tape, bare copper)

cable_armour_as_cpc._basis: Table 54.6 — SWA steel armour used as CPC

fault_levels_typical.domestic_LV_origin_kA: 1.0 to 6.0 (typical 1.5 for terraced, 4.0 for new estates)

MCB_above_Ia.note: Conservative — actual instantaneous trip time is 10-30ms. Use 0.1s for adiabatic calc unless manufacturer data states faster.

fuse_at_PSCC.note: HRC fuses trip in 10ms or less at high fault current. Read from manufacturer's t/I curve at the fault level.

MCCB_with_short_circuit_release.note: Read from manufacturer's published curve. Some have intentional time delay for selectivity.

worked_example.description: 2.5mm² T&E circuit, 32A Type B MCB, prospective fault current 3kA at the DB
worked_example._k_reasoning: Copper line conductor, PVC insulated (T&E), initial 70°C
worked_example.verdict: MARGINAL — at exactly 3kA, the cable can survive 9.2ms. Typical Type B MCB trips in 5-10ms. Acceptable but tight. For higher fault levels (5kA+), upsize cable or use faster fuse.

design_strategy.screening_check: For most domestic/small commercial work, fault levels at final circuits are low enough that the adiabatic check is automatically satisfied. Detailed calculation only needed for: large fault currents (>10kA), small cables on high-fault systems, long runs where R1+R2 may be deceptive.
design_strategy.when_in_doubt: Calculate. Cable failure during fault is a fire risk and a major liability.
design_strategy.shortcut_for_BS_EN_60898: For MCBs to BS EN 60898 protecting cables with k ≥ 115, if In ≤ Iz of the cable, the adiabatic check is satisfied at all fault currents the breaker will clear. Holds for typical UK domestic and small commercial.

selectivity_considerations.principle: Time delays added for selectivity (ZSI, S-type RCDs, time-graded MCCBs) increase disconnection time. The cable must survive the LONGER time.
selectivity_considerations.rule: When time delay is applied for selectivity, re-check adiabatic equation at the delayed time.
